name: Jewel Tide Flowing
description: The Jewel Tide Flowing, also known as Kanju or Manju, is one of the three sacred treasures of Japan, part of the Imperial Regalia. It is believed to be a magical jewel capable of controlling the tides, granting its wielder dominion over the sea. According to legend, the jewel was retrieved from the underwater palace of Ryūjin, the dragon god of the sea, by the trickster god Susanoo after he defeated the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi. The jewel's radiant light can summon and recede the ocean's waves at will, offering protection from invaders and control over maritime trade and travel. It symbolizes benevolence, prosperity, and the emperor's rightful rule, emphasizing their connection to the divine and the natural world. The Jewel Tide Flowing is not merely a beautiful object; it represents the power to nurture and sustain, reflecting the emperor's responsibility to safeguard the well-being of the nation. Its possession signifies the legitimate authority of the Imperial family and their ability to ensure the prosperity and safety of Japan through their connection to the kami and the natural forces of the world. The jewel's power is said to be linked to the spiritual purity of its holder, requiring a just and compassionate leader to wield its power effectively. Its loss would signify a catastrophic imbalance in the natural order and a crisis of leadership for the nation.
subtitle: Tidal control and Imperial power
origin: Retrieved from Ryūjin, the dragon god of the sea, by Susanoo.
currentLocation: Imperial Palace in Tokyo, Japan (believed to be)

keyMyths.title: Susanoo and the Serpent
keyMyths.description: Susanoo defeats Yamata no Orochi and discovers the Jewel Tide Flowing among the serpent's treasures, later presenting it to Amaterasu as a peace offering.

keyMyths.title: Empress Jingū's Conquest
keyMyths.description: Empress Jingū is said to have used the Jewel Tide Flowing to control the tides during her conquest of Korea, facilitating her naval invasion.

sources.author: Basil Hall Chamberlain
sources.description: Translation and commentary on the Kojiki, a primary source of Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and commentary on the Kojiki, a primary source of Japanese mythology.

sources.author: William George Aston

variants: Jewel of the Ebb and Flow
